7. Point Tracking

  1. Start with dense 2D motion between two nearby frames: optical flow.
  2. Realize dense flow is expensive and often ill-posed, so track only selected “good” points.
  3. Realize long-term tracking introduces drift, appearance change, and occlusion.
  4. Add stronger structure—either temporal consistency, tracking many points jointly, or 3D geometry.
  5. Move to harder matching problems where points may be far apart in space or viewpoint, and geometric verification like RANSAC becomes necessary.

7.1. Families of Motion Estimation

Point tracking is the other family to motion estimation where you track individual points

7.2. Sparse Motion Tracking

Setup: fixed camera, moving image, goal: compute motion

Idea of sparse motion tracking: some linear systems are better conditoned than others, e.g. tracking a white patch is worse than tracking a horizontal edge, which is worse than tracking a corner.

[COOL] Find sparse points with SIFT.

SIFT (Scale-invariant feature transform)
Detects distinctive keypoints in an image and computes descriptors (vectors) for them so the same points can be matched across images despite changes in scale, rotation, and moderately viewpoint or lighting. The original paper by D Lowe has 84k citations! The mechanism is

  • Find keypoints by looking at points prominent in consecutive Laplacian pyramid levels (edge/blob detector). Candidates are local extremums in the neighborhood
  • Refine and filter candidates (e.g. Harris corner measure):
  • Assign a direction by computing gradients in all pixeils around the neighbohood in a 36-bin histogram.
  • The direction is used to canonicalize the patch and form the keypoint descriptor.

  • To match keypoints between images, find which keypoint descriptor in the other image. is nearest in L2 distance

    • Lowe’s Ratio Test: For each keypoint in A, find its two nearest neighbors in B. Accept the match only if: distance(best) / distance(second_best) < threshold (typically 0.75)

[REVIEW] The structure tensor is a 2x2 matrix computed at each pixel from a local neighborhood around that pixel that captures image variance along all directions. Math fact: you can compute it by computing the partial derivatives and convoling it.

where denotes partial derivative. If both eigienvalues are large, flow is reliable. If one is small we have the aperture problem. If both are small we have no signal.

Elaboration on eigenvalues and Relation to Lucas-Kanade

We can use the eigenvalues of the structure tensor as a criterion for selecting good features e.g. Harris corners and Shi Tomasi criterion:

Detecting and tracking

  • High level Idea: just detect these points each frame and then for each poitn compute its flow via Lucas-Kanade in a local window (recall this works pointwise.)
  • This can be fooled by appearance changes, e.g. lighting changes where the obect moves. The fix is to maintain a dynamical model of the pose, lighting, and couple temporally?‘

Particle video (2008) is a technqiue that lies between too sparse (Shi Tomasi criterion) and too dense (optical flow) and works on long range

[TODO] Persistent Independent Particles (PIP) tracks a single point over many frames (cf Raft which tracks all points between two frames.)

  • Pick one point in one frame. The model guesses where that same point is in every frame of the video (by comparing dense CNN embeddings), then repeatedly corrects those guesses in a sliding window. It also keeps a learned description of what the point looks like and predicts whether the point is visible or occluded.

[TODO] CoTracker: Extends PIP to many points. Instead of tracking each point independently, it lets the points share information. That helps because nearby points on the same object usually move together, and visible points can help infer where occluded points went.

Idea: Store proxy tokens or registers that aren’t associated with points but the scene.

7.3. Structure from Motion

Setup: fixed 3D object, multiple perspectives, e.g. from moving camera, output sparse 3D point cloud

Scene Flow (3D analog of optical flow) deterministically relates Optical Flow, Camera Motion, and Depth. I.e. given any two of these, you can derive the third. For example, if you know the camera didn’t move and you know the depth, you can predict exactly what the optical flow should look like.

Structure from Motion (SfM) for a static scene: Assume static 3D scene. Input: Video + optical flow + sparse points, and the output is depth map+ camera parameters for each frame. Loss is a camera-induced flow loss (ensure consistency between optical flow and the flow that the camera motion and depth induces). THe loss function for a pair of frames looks like , e.g. you can emphasize consistency more for the sparse points.

  • FlowMap is a NN architecture for this.
